Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Early Modern

Mary Shepherd

1777 – 1847

A rigorous realist reply to Berkeley and Hume: causation, mind, and the external world.

Lady Mary Shepherd’s thought on mind is not a minor appendix to her metaphysics. It is one of the main places where her anti-Humean causal realism does its deepest work. Her philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a causal, dispositional, anti-skeptical, anti-materialist, and non-Cartesian account of consciousness, perception, selfhood, embodiment, agency, and immortality. She treats mind as a real power or capacity for sensation, thought, and feeling; she treats the body and nervous system as indispensable causal conditions for ordinary human consciousness; she rejects the reduction of mind to organization or brain; and she argues that both external bodies and enduring minds are known through reason’s grasp of necessary causal relations, not through mere habit, instinct, or direct inspection of hidden essences.

A compact statement of the system would be this: Shepherd thinks that every conscious state is an effect requiring a cause; that the stream of sensations cannot be self-subsisting; that there must be an enduring subject or capacity capable of being successively modified; that the bodily organism helps elicit and individuate human mental life without being identical to mind; that perception is a rational interpretation of sensations as signs of external causes; that the self is not a mere bundle of perceptions but an embodied, memory-bearing, causally continuous being; and that the “soul” or individual mind is immaterial in the sense that it is not reducible to solid extension, brain, or organization, though she is cautious about what philosophy can prove concerning post-mortem personal consciousness.

1. Corpus and philosophical setting

The core texts for Shepherd’s philosophy of mind are An Essay upon the Relation of Cause and Effect of 1824 and Essays on the Perception of an External Universe of 1827. The first supplies the metaphysical theory of causation; the second applies it to perception, external objects, mind, body, self, soul, divine mind, and skeptical challenges. The Essay upon the Relation of Cause and Effect is explicitly directed against Hume and Thomas Brown on causation, while Essays on the Perception of an External Universe is directed especially against Berkeleyan idealism, Humean skepticism, Reid’s appeal to instinctive belief, and materialist or physiological reductions of life and sentience.

Her system is best read as a metaphysics of mind rather than a merely empirical psychology. Nevertheless, she does have a psychology: she analyzes sensation, consciousness, perception, memory, association, belief, agency, sleep, dreams, bodily action, animal sentience, and personal identity. The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes her broader position as a systematic metaphysics and epistemology in which sensation and understanding jointly yield perceptual knowledge, and in which causality is central to knowledge of external objects and objects’ causal powers.

The historical target is crucial. Shepherd wants to block a line of development she sees running from Hume’s theory of causation and custom, through Brown’s philosophy, into William Lawrence’s materialist medicine and anti-vitalist account of life. In the Lawrence controversy, “life” was treated as the actions of living bodies, with no need for a soul as cause of life; Shepherd’s response was not merely theological but metaphysical: she attacks the causal theory that allows such reduction.

2. The causal metaphysics beneath her philosophy of mind

Shepherd’s philosophy of mind cannot be extracted without first stating her theory of causation. Against Hume, she denies that causation is merely constant conjunction plus habit. She argues that reason, not custom, gives us the principle that every beginning of existence must have a cause and that like causes must produce like effects. Her project is to show that causal necessity is not projected by the mind onto the world, but is rooted in the nature of things.

Her mature definition of cause is not simply “one event followed by another.” A cause is a set or union of qualities whose conjunction produces a new quality or object. She repeatedly uses the idea of a “mixture” or “compound mass”: when A and B combine, their union is C. The effect is not an arbitrary later event but the new nature generated by the causal conjunction. This is why Shepherd often sounds closer to a theory of powers or dispositional essences than to Humean event regularity.

This causal doctrine matters for mind because mental states are not exceptions to causality. Sensations, thoughts, volitions, perceptions, and memories are beginnings or modifications; as such, they require causes. There can be no free-floating sensation, no self-caused idea, no ungrounded perception, and no spontaneous beginning of consciousness without a producing power. Her philosophy of mind is therefore built from one governing axiom: mental phenomena are real effects, and real effects require real causes.

This makes Shepherd a causal realist about psychology. A mental state is not merely something that appears; it is something produced. The causes of a conscious state may include the mind’s own capacity for sensation, the bodily organs, the brain, the external object, prior mental states, memory traces, and divine or general laws of nature. But a conscious state is never an ultimate brute datum in the skeptical or Humean sense. It is a sign of a causal structure.

3. “Sensation” as the broad genus of consciousness

A major difficulty in reading Shepherd is that she uses “sensation” far more broadly than modern philosophers usually do. She explicitly uses it as a generic term for every kind of consciousness: thought, idea, feeling, perception, memory, and inward awareness. She says that no perception of exterior objects is possible without an inward act of consciousness, and that every thought, notion, idea, feeling, or perception may be counted, in the most general sense, as a sensation.

This does not mean that Shepherd is a crude sensationalist who reduces all cognition to passive sensory impressions. On the contrary, she distinguishes sensations as conscious effects from the understanding’s activity in comparing, relating, and interpreting them. “Sensation” names the whole field of conscious occurrence; “perception” is a more structured act in which a sensation is noticed, compared, and understood. The Stanford Encyclopedia captures this point by noting that, for Shepherd, perception is a consciousness of sensation, but also an observation of relations among ideas and a kind of simple inference by the understanding.

This usage gives Shepherd a distinctive psychology. Consciousness is not divided at the outset into sharp compartments of sensation, idea, judgment, and feeling. Instead, all these are conscious modifications of a sentient capacity. The philosophical problem is then to explain how such modifications are caused, how they refer beyond themselves, and what enduring subject must exist for them to occur successively.

4. Perception: consciousness plus latent reasoning

Shepherd’s theory of perception is an anti-skeptical transformation of the empiricist tradition. She grants a premise that might seem to favor Hume or Berkeley: everything immediately present to consciousness is a sensation or idea. But she denies that this traps us inside ideas. Sensations are effects, and effects require causes. Since many sensations arise in orderly, lawlike, involuntary patterns that depend on bodily motion and resistance, reason judges that their causes are external to the mind.

The external world is therefore known neither by direct naïve realism nor by blind instinct. Against Reid, Shepherd rejects the idea that belief in external objects is simply an inexplicable “original principle” or instinct of common sense. Against Hume, she rejects the idea that belief arises merely from custom or association. Against Berkeley, she rejects the collapse of sensible reality into ideas. Perception is a rational, though often implicit, interpretation of sensations as signs of causes beyond themselves.

Her theory is sometimes close to what later philosophers would call indirect realism, critical realism, or structural causal realism. We do not know external bodies as bare things-in-themselves. We know them through the stable relations among our sensations, because those relations indicate stable relations among their causes. The mind reads sensations somewhat as algebraic signs or diorama-like effects: the signs are mental, but the order among the signs gives knowledge of the order among their causes.

This point is decisive for her philosophy of mind. The mind is not a passive theater in which impressions appear. It is an active rational capacity that detects causal relations, distinguishes inward effects from outward causes, and thereby converts sensation into objective perception. Perception is psychologically immediate in ordinary life, but philosophically analyzable as sensation plus rational causal judgment.

5. Inward and outward: the mind’s place in perception

Shepherd’s distinction between “inward” and “outward” is subtle. Sensations exist only in the mind, but the causes of many sensations must be outside the mind. She defines inward existence as the capacity for sensation in general, and outward existence as the exciting cause of some particular sensation. The mind is thus the power of thought and feeling; the outward object is the motive or cause that elicits a particular kind of thought or feeling.

This means that the sensible qualities we experience—colors, sounds, tactile feels, and so on—are not literally qualities inhering in external objects as they appear to us. They are effects in the mind. External objects are known as causal powers capable of producing these effects under appropriate bodily and environmental conditions. Shepherd is therefore not a naïve realist about sensible qualities. Her realism is causal and relational.

At the same time, she does not retreat into skepticism. The fact that sensible qualities are mental effects does not show that external objects are unknowable. It shows that they are known through their effects. Because every new conscious effect requires a cause, and because ordered effects require ordered causes, the mind is rationally entitled to infer an external universe. Perceptual knowledge is therefore mediated by sensation but grounded in necessary causal principles.

6. Dreams, hallucination, and skeptical pressure

Shepherd is aware that dreams and hallucination threaten her argument. If dream sensations can resemble waking sensations, why infer an external world from ordinary experience? Her response is that dream experiences do not possess the same systematic order, practical coordination, and dependence on bodily motion as waking perceptions. Dreams may produce internally coherent episodes, but they do not sustain the full network of lawlike relations that waking perception exhibits.

Her answer is not that dreams are impossible or always transparently different from waking life. Rather, it is that the total structure of waking experience gives rational grounds for distinguishing sensations caused by external objects from sensations caused by other internal conditions. This gives her theory an early form of what might now be called a holistic or coherence-based defense of perceptual realism, though anchored in causal necessity rather than mere coherence.

7. The ontology of mind: capacity, not bundle

Shepherd’s central ontological claim is that mind is the capacity or power for sensation in general. It is not identical with any one sensation, nor with the passing series of sensations. Each sensation comes and goes; the mind is the continuing subject or capacity capable of being successively modified.

This is her answer to Hume’s bundle theory. If the self were only a bundle of perceptions, then there would be no enduring subject in which the bundle occurs. But every changing sensation must be a change of something. Since sensations vanish and new sensations arise, there must be a continuing power or subject capable of receiving or producing them. The “I” is therefore not an impression among impressions; it is an abstract way of referring to the being that remains exterior to, and independent of, each particular mental change.

This does not make Shepherd a straightforward Cartesian substance dualist. She does not claim to know the real essence of mind as Descartes claims to know mind as thinking substance. She defines mind functionally and causally: mind is what has the power to feel, think, and be conscious. Its hidden essence remains unknown. In this respect, she is closer to a dispositional or powers ontology than to a classical substance ontology.

8. Mind, body, and the rejection of reductive materialism

Shepherd sharply distinguishes mind from body. Body is the continuing exciting cause of perceptions of extension and solidity; mind is the capacity or cause for sensation in general. The two are essentially different in their known powers, even though we do not know their ultimate essences.

Yet she does not deny that human consciousness depends, in ordinary life, on bodily organization. She repeatedly grants that sensations as we know them are connected with the brain, nerves, sense organs, and living organization. The body is not a dispensable accident. It is an indispensable causal partner in ordinary human perception, memory, and action.

Her anti-materialism is therefore not anti-physiological. She does not deny that the brain and organs are involved in producing conscious states. She denies that organization alone explains the original power of sentience. A living nerve may be a condition of sensation, but it is not the whole cause of sensation. The capacity to feel cannot be reduced to the arrangement of insentient matter unless one can show how the power of consciousness is generated by extension and solidity alone. Shepherd thinks that such a reduction simply assigns the effect to an insufficient cause.

This is the core of her dispute with materialist physiology. Lawrence and similar writers could explain many bodily functions in bodily terms, but Shepherd thinks they mistake the organization that elicits or modifies sentience for the metaphysical ground of sentience itself. Organization changes how powers operate; it does not create the original power to feel from nothing.

9. A non-Cartesian dualism of powers

Shepherd’s position is dualist, but not in the most familiar Cartesian form. She does not begin with two transparent substances, res cogitans and res extensa. She begins with two families of powers known through effects: material powers such as extension, solidity, motion, resistance, and organization; and mental powers such as sensation, thought, feeling, knowledge, choice, and will.

Because all causation is a conjunction of powers producing new effects, Shepherd does not see mind-body interaction as uniquely mysterious. Mind and body can coalesce into one causal compound, producing conscious perception or voluntary motion. A mental act does not have to push matter by a mechanically intelligible impulse; it can be one constituent in a complex cause whose total effect is bodily motion or conscious experience.

This makes her interactionism unusual. She is not an occasionalist; she does not make God the immediate substitute cause of every mind-body relation. She is not a materialist; she does not identify mental powers with bodily organization. She is not a Cartesian who treats interaction as an embarrassing anomaly. She treats mind-body interaction as one instance of a general metaphysics of causal combination.

10. Mental causation, will, and agency

Shepherd’s account of agency follows naturally from her metaphysics. Intentions, designs, perceptions, knowledge of ends, and choices are real causal factors. When an agent acts, the mental state is not epiphenomenal. It helps begin and direct bodily motion toward an end. She repeatedly treats perception and will as joined with material conditions in the production of action.

This gives Shepherd a robust theory of mental causation. The bodily mechanism may execute motion, but the direction toward an end requires knowledge, design, and choice. Artifacts, purposive bodily acts, and intelligent coordination cannot be fully explained by matter in motion unless the explanatory story includes mental causes. Recent scholarship has therefore described her as “naturalizing” mind by folding it into the class of causally governed natural phenomena, while still resisting reduction of mind to matter.

Her position also has compatibilist implications. If every beginning requires a cause, then voluntary actions are necessarily caused. But that does not remove agency, because agency consists partly in the agent’s own mental states serving as causes. Freedom is not uncaused spontaneity; it is action arising from the agent’s own perceptions, intentions, and choices within a necessary causal order. This is why recent work treats Shepherd as developing a psychology of intention that answers worries about sourcehood in a deterministic universe.

11. Association of ideas

Shepherd does not treat association as Hume does. For Hume, association and custom explain why the mind comes to expect one event after another. Shepherd thinks that association is real but cannot be the foundation of causal knowledge. Causal knowledge rests on reason’s grasp of necessary relations, not on habituated expectation.

In her positive psychology, association is a compound mental state: one idea or state of mind becomes united with another so that the mind passes from one to the other. But this associative transition itself must be causally explained. It is not a primitive magic by which custom creates necessity. Association is one psychological mechanism within an already causal mind; it is not the origin of causality itself.

This is an important difference from Humean psychology. Shepherd accepts that repeated experience shapes expectation, memory, and thought. But repetition is not what makes causation necessary. Rather, repetition allows us to discover stable causal powers whose necessity is intelligible by reason.

12. The self and personal identity

Shepherd distinguishes, or at least allows us to distinguish, mind from self. Mind in the narrow sense is the capacity for sensation in general. The self is the more complex being constituted by that capacity together with bodily organization, present consciousness, memory, and the continuing powers that produce a sense of personal existence.

She explicitly rejects the idea that personal identity depends on sameness of material particles. The body changes its matter, but identity persists through continuity of powers, memory, and present consciousness. The sense of self arises from the union of remembered ideas with present impressions, not from an unchanging lump of matter.

This makes her position intermediate between Locke and Hume. Like Locke, she gives memory a central role in personal identity. Unlike Hume, she refuses to reduce the self to a bundle of perceptions. Unlike a crude bodily theory, she denies that identity consists in sameness of particles. The self is an enduring causal unity whose psychological continuity is expressed through memory and consciousness, and whose ordinary individuation is embodied.

There is a scholarly dispute here. Deborah Boyle emphasizes that Shepherd often uses “mind,” “soul,” and “self” in related or overlapping ways, with broader and narrower senses. Manuel Fasko argues more sharply that the self is an effect of causal interactions among mind, body, present consciousness, memory, and their union. The best reconstruction is to say that Shepherd’s terminology is flexible: “mind” names the basic sentient capacity; “soul” often names that capacity under its immaterial or religious aspect; “self” names the individuated, memory-bearing, embodied subject of personal life.

13. Unconsciousness, sleep, and the persistence of mind

Shepherd’s account of sleep is philosophically important. If mind were identical with occurrent consciousness, then dreamless sleep would interrupt or destroy the self. Shepherd denies this. The self can exist during unconscious sleep because mind is not an occurrent sensation but a capacity for sensation.

This is one of her strongest arguments against bundle theories. A bundle theory has difficulty explaining the persistence of the subject through gaps in consciousness. Shepherd’s dispositional account handles the case directly: the power to feel may persist even when it is not currently exercised. Just as a causal power may exist when inactive, mind may exist when not conscious.

The implication is significant: consciousness is an operation or manifestation of mind, not the whole being of mind. This gives Shepherd a distinction that later philosophy of mind would express as the distinction between a categorical or dispositional subject and its occurrent mental states.

14. The soul and incorporeal mind

Shepherd’s remarks on soul and immortality are not incidental. They are integrated into her causal metaphysics. If every sensation is a transient change, and if every change requires a continuing subject or capacity, then mind must be a continuous being capable of successive sensations. She argues that the organs of sense are not the essence of the being that feels; they elicit and modify sensation, but they are not the original power of sentience.

She therefore argues for the immateriality of mind in a qualified sense. Mind is not matter understood as solid extension. Sensation is not extension or solidity. The power to feel is different in kind from the powers by which bodies occupy space, resist pressure, or move. Yet Shepherd does not give a simple doctrine of an aspatial Cartesian soul. She sometimes suggests that sensation, though unextended as a quality, must have some relation to space or to an underlying subject. Her immaterialism is thus better described as anti-reductionist and anti-materialist rather than as a fully worked-out doctrine of an immaterial substance with a known essence.

On immortality, Shepherd is bolder and more cautious at the same time. She argues that mind, as the continuous subject or power of sensation, is not generated by the body in the way materialists suppose. She even speaks of an eternal power of feeling conveyed to each animal and treats mind as having no beginning in the ordinary material sense. But she is cautious about whether philosophy alone can prove the preservation of individual personality, memory, and the familiar structure of self after death.

This distinction is essential. Shepherd thinks metaphysics can establish the continuing or eternal character of mind as a sentient power. But whether the post-mortem mind retains memory, personality, and recognizable selfhood is less demonstrable. She regards resurrection or future consciousness as philosophically possible and analogically probable, and she allows religious revelation to carry weight where metaphysical demonstration reaches its limit.

15. Divine mind and universal consciousness

Shepherd’s philosophy of mind extends from finite mind to divine mind. Her causal realism leads her to infer a universal mind from purposive order, directed motion, and the existence of means fitted to ends. She argues that mental qualities such as perception, knowledge, and direction are required to explain the ordered production of effects.

The divine mind differs from finite minds because it does not require organs. Finite creatures need organs to elicit and modify particular sensations; God, as self-existent eternal mind, does not. Divine perception is not dependent on eyes, nerves, brain, or bodily organization. This allows Shepherd to use the same general concept of mind—capacity for perception, knowledge, and direction—while distinguishing finite embodied minds from the infinite universal mind.

Her theology is not a detachable ornament. It functions as part of the final architecture of her metaphysics. She argues that matter, motion, mind, and consciousness require an ultimate causal ground. But she also says that her purpose in these essays is to answer atheistical opinions rather than to present a complete theology.

16. Animal minds and degrees of sentience

Shepherd’s account is not restricted to adult human rational consciousness. Because mind is the capacity for sensation, any creature capable of sensation has some form of mind. The question then becomes how different bodily organizations elicit different ranges of sensation, memory, action, and perhaps selfhood. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that Shepherd applies her account broadly to sentient organisms, and recent scholarship emphasizes that individual human minds must be distinguished from mind considered in abstraction.

This matters because it shows that Shepherd’s theory is not merely an introspective theory of human self-consciousness. It is a general metaphysics of sentience. Human minds are special because of their bodily organization, memory, reasoning, language, and moral agency; they are not special because only humans possess the basic power of sensation.

17. Her implicit psychology of cognition

Shepherd’s psychology can be reconstructed around several faculties or operations, though she does not present them as a modern faculty psychology in systematic textbook form.

First, sensation is the generic conscious modification. It includes not only external sensory feeling but thought, idea, perception, and inward feeling.

Second, understanding is the power by which relations among sensations or ideas are noticed. The understanding grasps causal implications: a beginning requires a cause; ordered effects imply ordered causes; like causes produce like effects.

Third, memory preserves or revives past ideas and unites them with present consciousness to generate personal identity. Identity is therefore not particle sameness but continuity of powers and remembered consciousness.

Fourth, association explains transitions among ideas, but it is subordinate to causality rather than foundational for it. Association is a real psychological fact, not the metaphysical source of necessary connection.

Fifth, will or choice is a mental cause that helps direct bodily motion toward perceived ends. It is causally necessary, but it is still genuinely agential because the agent’s own mental states are part of the producing cause.

Sixth, belief is neither brute instinct nor custom alone. It is the mind’s rational assent to causal relations disclosed in experience. Belief in the external world is thus a rational achievement, even if ordinarily implicit and automatic.

18. Her relation to Hume

Hume is Shepherd’s central adversary. She accepts the seriousness of his challenge: if all we ever observe are perceptions, and if causation is reduced to habit, then knowledge of external bodies, enduring selves, divine mind, and necessary connection appears threatened. But she rejects nearly every major Humean conclusion.

Against Hume on causation, she argues that necessary connection is intelligible because an effect is the product of a causal union. Against Hume on induction, she argues that like causes must produce like effects because the same causal union cannot produce different results without some difference in the cause. Against Hume on selfhood, she argues that a succession of perceptions requires a continuing subject. Against Hume on external objects, she argues that sensations are effects whose causes must be distinguished from the sensations themselves.

Her philosophy of mind can therefore be read as a systematic attempt to repair empiricism after Hume without retreating to dogmatic rationalism. She keeps the empiricist premise that consciousness gives us sensations, but she adds that reason necessarily interprets sensations causally.

19. Her relation to Berkeley

Shepherd takes Berkeley seriously because Berkeley correctly sees that sensible qualities exist in consciousness. But she denies that this entails idealism. For Shepherd, sensible qualities are mental effects, but effects require causes; the cause of a sensation need not resemble the sensation. External objects are therefore not collections of ideas but unknown powers that produce ideas under lawful conditions.

This makes her anti-Berkeleyan realism unusually sophisticated. She does not try to restore naïve resemblance realism. She does not say that colors and sounds exist outside the mind exactly as they appear. Instead, she says that the external world exists as the causal ground of the ordered sensory field. This is a realist theory, but not a simple copy theory.

20. Her relation to Reid and Scottish common sense

Shepherd shares Reid’s anti-skeptical aim but rejects his method. Reid had appealed to natural principles or instincts of belief to secure trust in the external world. Shepherd thinks this gives up too much to skepticism because it does not explain why such belief is rational. She wants to show not merely that we naturally believe in external objects, but that reason justifies the belief.

In this respect, she occupies a distinctive position within post-Humean Scottish philosophy. She is a common-sense realist in aim, but not in method. Her method is metaphysical and causal, not merely descriptive of irresistible belief.

21. Her relation to Locke

Shepherd inherits from Locke an interest in ideas, sensation, reflection, and personal identity. Like Locke, she treats memory as central to the self. Like Locke, she distinguishes real essence from nominal or known qualities. But she goes beyond Locke by giving a much stronger causal proof of external objects and a much stronger anti-Humean account of necessary connection.

Her theory of mind also modifies Locke’s psychology. Locke’s account sometimes leaves the relation between ideas, substances, powers, and personal identity underdetermined. Shepherd tries to close those gaps by insisting that sensations are effects, that powers are real, and that a continuous subject is required for successive consciousness.

22. Her relation to Descartes

Shepherd is often dualist in conclusion but not Cartesian in foundation. Descartes begins from the cogito and defines mind as thinking substance. Shepherd begins from causal principles and defines mind as the capacity for sensation, thought, and feeling. Descartes makes the essence of body extension and the essence of mind thought; Shepherd is more cautious, treating both body and mind as known through powers while their real essences remain hidden.

She also avoids the standard Cartesian interaction problem by embedding mind-body union in her general theory of causal mixture. If causation always involves the combination of powers producing new effects, then there is no special scandal in mental and material powers jointly producing perception or action.

Thus, if she is called a dualist, the best label is not “substance dualist” without qualification. Better labels would be “causal-power dualist,” “dispositional dualist,” or “non-reductive interactionist.” These are modern labels, but they capture her distinctive refusal to reduce mind to matter while also refusing to define mind by a transparent Cartesian essence.

23. Her relation to early nineteenth-century materialism and physiology

Shepherd’s immediate historical importance lies partly in her response to early nineteenth-century physiological materialism. William Lawrence’s lectures and writings treated life and bodily function in ways that seemed to exclude soul or vital principle. Shepherd saw this as philosophically connected to Humean and Brownian views of causation. If causation is only regular sequence, then one may treat bodily organization as followed by life and consciousness without explaining how organization produces sentience.

Shepherd’s answer is that this is not enough. A genuine cause must contain powers adequate to the effect. If consciousness appears, the cause must include a capacity for consciousness. Insentient extension and organization cannot, merely by being arranged, explain the beginning of sensation unless one identifies a power of sentience in the cause.

This makes her an important figure in the prehistory of later debates about emergence and reduction. Recent scholarship has explicitly connected her metaphysics to emergentist themes: new qualities arise from causal unions, and wholes may possess powers not evident in isolated components. But Shepherd herself uses this idea to resist reductive materialism, not to dissolve mind into matter.

24. Systematic reconstruction: Shepherd’s philosophy of mind in theses

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Shepherd can be stated in the following theses.

First, consciousness is real occurrence. Sensations, thoughts, ideas, feelings, perceptions, memories, and volitions are not fictions or mere verbal habits. They are real conscious modifications.

Second, every conscious modification requires a cause. No sensation begins itself. This follows from her general causal principle that every beginning of existence must have a producing cause.

Third, the mind is the enduring capacity for conscious modification. Mind is not identical with the current stream of consciousness; it is the power or subject capable of successive sensations.

Fourth, the body is a causal partner in ordinary human consciousness. The brain, nerves, organs, and bodily motions help elicit, structure, and vary sensations. But they are not identical with the original power of sentience.

Fifth, perception is sensation interpreted by causal reason. The mind knows external objects by treating sensations as effects and signs of external causes.

Sixth, external objects are known as causal powers, not as exact replicas of sensible qualities. Colors, sounds, and other sensible qualities exist as mental effects; external bodies are the causes that produce them under appropriate conditions.

Seventh, mental causation is genuine. Intentions, choices, perceptions, and designs help produce bodily action. The will is not epiphenomenal.

Eighth, agency is compatible with causal necessity. Voluntary action is caused, but it is caused partly by the agent’s own mental states. Shepherd’s view is therefore closer to compatibilism than to libertarian uncaused choice.

Ninth, personal identity is not sameness of material particles. It consists in continuity of powers, memory, present consciousness, and embodied organization.

Tenth, the soul or mind is immaterial in the sense that it is not reducible to solid extension, matter, or bodily organization. But Shepherd does not claim full knowledge of the soul’s hidden essence.

Eleventh, mind may persist without occurrent consciousness. Dreamless sleep already shows that the subject can continue when consciousness is not active. This supports her broader argument that mind is a capacity, not a bundle.

Twelfth, immortality is partly demonstrable and partly speculative. The continued reality of mind as a power is, for Shepherd, metaphysically grounded; the retention of individual memory and personality after death is probable or religiously supported rather than fully demonstrable by philosophy alone.

25. Tensions and unresolved problems

The first major tension is the status of the mind’s “essence.” Shepherd insists that mind is not matter, but she also denies that we know real essences directly. This raises a question: does she prove that mind is a distinct kind of substance, or only that the power of sentience is not reducible to known material powers? The second formulation is safer and more faithful to her method.

The second tension concerns embodiment. She sometimes speaks as if mind is an eternal power that can survive bodily dissolution. Yet her account of the individuated self depends heavily on body, brain, memory, and present consciousness. This creates a gap between survival of mind and survival of person. Shepherd sees the gap and does not pretend that metaphysics alone fully closes it.

The third tension concerns the causal explanation of consciousness. Shepherd argues that bodily organization alone cannot explain sentience because the cause must be adequate to the effect. But critics may ask whether postulating a power of sentience explains consciousness or merely names the required explanans. Her answer would be that all causal explanation eventually appeals to powers known through their effects; mind is no worse off than gravity, solidity, or chemical affinity in this respect.

The fourth tension concerns determinism and responsibility. If every action is necessarily caused, then moral responsibility must be compatibilist. Shepherd’s resources for this are substantial: the agent’s own perceptions, judgments, and choices are causes. But she does not develop a full moral psychology of responsibility at the same level of detail as her account of perception and causation.

26. Historical importance for philosophy of mind

Shepherd deserves a more central place in the history of philosophy of mind than she has usually received. She stands at the intersection of early modern debates about ideas, substances, and personal identity; Scottish common-sense responses to skepticism; nineteenth-century physiology and materialism; and later questions about mental causation, emergence, and non-reductive realism.

Her historical significance is fourfold. First, she offers one of the strongest early nineteenth-century replies to Hume’s bundle theory of mind and custom-based account of causation. Second, she gives a sophisticated causal theory of perception that avoids both Berkeleyan idealism and naïve realism. Third, she formulates a non-reductive theory of mind-body interaction that treats mental causes as real within nature. Fourth, she anticipates later debates about whether consciousness can be reduced to organization, whether mental states are causally efficacious, and whether personal identity requires more than psychological continuity.

She also had a nontrivial nineteenth-century reception. Paoletti notes that William Whewell used one of her works as a Cambridge textbook, that Robert Blakey discussed her in a history of the philosophy of mind, and that Coleridge praised her. This matters because Shepherd was not merely a private eccentric later rediscovered; she was part of the philosophical conversation about causality, mind, and science in her own century.

27. Final classification

The most accurate classification of Shepherd’s philosophy of mind is this: she is a causal-power theorist of mind, a non-reductive interactionist, an anti-Humean realist about perception and self, an anti-materialist about sentience, and a cautious defender of the soul’s immortality.

She is not an idealist, because external causes are real. She is not a materialist, because sentience is not reducible to extension or organization. She is not a simple Cartesian, because mind is defined by causal capacity rather than by a transparent substance-essence known through pure introspection. She is not a Humean, because necessity, self, external world, and mind are rationally grounded. She is not merely a common-sense philosopher, because she does not rest content with instinctive belief. Her distinctive achievement is to make mind intelligible within a universal causal metaphysics while preserving the irreducibility of consciousness, agency, and personhood.

A comprehensive Shepherdian philosophy of mind would therefore begin with this proposition: mind is the enduring causal power for consciousness, known through the effects it produces and the rational necessity that every conscious change must have a subject and cause. From that proposition follow her theories of perception, embodiment, agency, selfhood, soul, and divine mind. Her system is not complete by contemporary standards, but it is rigorous, ambitious, and historically important: it offers a powerful nineteenth-century alternative to both Humean psychological naturalism and reductive physiological materialism.

PreviousDavid Hume NextImmanuel Kant

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
